meta.name: Suspected LVO Stroke — Right-Sided Weakness with Global Aphasia, LKW 45 Minutes

patient.name: Harold Pemberton
patient.chiefComplaint: Sudden right-sided weakness and inability to speak; last known well 45 minutes ago

history.hpi: 72-year-old retired engineer was eating breakfast with his wife and reading the newspaper at 09:15 when she observed him drop his coffee cup, develop an obvious right facial droop, and stop responding when she spoke to him. She helped him to the couch, called 911 within 2 minutes, and EMS is on scene now (45 minutes after symptom onset). Patient is awake, follows simple commands by gesture, but cannot produce intelligible speech and cannot move his right arm or leg. No reported headache, seizure activity, vomiting, head trauma, or recent illness. Patient takes warfarin for atrial fibrillation and his last INR (yesterday) was 2.4 per his wife. Wife was sitting across from him the entire time and is certain of the time of onset.
medications: Warfarin 5 mg PO daily (last INR 2.4 yesterday)
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee and a few bites of toast at 09:00, dropped at 09:15
history.events: Wife witnessed sudden right-sided weakness and loss of speech at 09:15 while patient was reading the paper. 911 called immediately. EMS arrival 45 minutes after symptom onset.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 72-year-old male, possible stroke, right-sided weakness and trouble speaking. Wife on scene, time of onset known.
scene.location: Single-family home, dining room. Patient now seated on couch in adjacent living room. Adequate lighting and access; clear path to stretcher.
scene.safety: Scene secure. No environmental hazards. Wife is composed and an excellent historian.
scene.bystanders: Wife (primary historian) was directly across from patient at the breakfast table. She is certain symptoms began at exactly 09:15 — she looked at the wall clock immediately. No prior episodes today. Patient on warfarin with recent INR of 2.4. No known recent trauma, surgery, or bleeding.
visualCues: Patient seated on couch, leaning slightly to the right
Obvious right facial droop on resting observation
Right arm hanging limp at his side
Newspaper and overturned coffee cup with spill on the table
Patient making frustrated facial expressions and grunting attempts at speech
Pill organizer visible on counter labeled with the days of the week
Wall clock visible in dining area

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives 45 minutes after a witnessed sudden onset of right-sided weakness and aphasia in a 72-year-old anticoagulated male. He is awake but unable to speak, with dense right hemiparesis and a marked facial droop. Wife provides a precise last-known-well time. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and a focused stroke assessment drive the response.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Regular, unlabored respirations; clear bilateral lung sounds
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Irregularly irregular radial pulse; warm, dry skin; no peripheral edema
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Equal at 3 mm, briskly reactive; conjugate gaze with mild forced left gaze deviation (cannot voluntarily cross midline to the right)
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Dense right hemiparesis: right arm drifts and falls immediately when raised; right leg cannot lift off bed against gravity (0/5); left side 5/5 strength. Right facial droop with obvious nasolabial flattening.
otherFindings: Global aphasia: cannot follow two-step verbal commands; cannot name objects; cannot repeat phrases
Sensory neglect of the right side suspected
No headache reported by gesture (patient shakes head 'no' to head pain)
No tongue laceration, no incontinence, no recent fall
Pulse irregularly irregular consistent with known atrial fibrillation
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Unable to produce intelligible speech; grunts and makes one-word attempts only ('no... no...').

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish exact last known well time (09:15 — wall clock)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Determine 'last known well' time. Key Documentation Elements: 'Last known well' must be specific. If the patient was last known well prior to bedtime the night before, this is the time to be documented (not time the patient woke up with symptoms present).
expectedActions.rationale: LKW determines thrombolytic and thrombectomy eligibility — the single most consequential data point in stroke care.

expectedActions.shortAction: Perform validated stroke scale (Cincinnati: face, arm, speech)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use a validated prehospital stroke scale that may include, but is not limited to: Facial smile/grimace – ask patient to smile; Arm drift – close eyes and hold out arms for count of 10 seconds; Speech – ask patient to say 'You can't teach an old dog new tricks'.
expectedActions.rationale: Documents positive findings (face droop, arm drift, aphasia) that activate the stroke alert.

expectedActions.shortAction: Stroke severity scale: assess vision, neglect, aphasia (LVO suspected)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use a validated prehospital stroke severity scale that may include, but is not limited to: Vision changes; Sensory neglect; Aphasia.
expectedActions.rationale: LVO findings (forced gaze, dense hemiparesis, global aphasia, neglect) drive thrombectomy-capable destination decision.

expectedActions.shortAction: Check blood glucose (rule out hypoglycemia mimic)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Check blood glucose level (BGL). Treat only if glucose less than 60 mg/dL.
expectedActions.rationale: Hypoglycemia is the classic stroke mimic and must be excluded before stroke alert.

expectedActions.shortAction: Document warfarin use, last INR, allergies, recent trauma/surgery/bleed
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pertinent historical data includes: Patient is taking warfarin or any anticoagulant medication; History of recent trauma; History of recent seizure; History of recent surgery; History of recent hemorrhage (e.g., GI bleed).
expectedActions.rationale: Anticoagulation status changes thrombolytic eligibility and changes hospital management priority.

expectedActions.shortAction: Oxygen as appropriate to maintain SpO2 94-98%
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Administer oxygen as appropriate with a target of achieving 94–98% saturation.
expectedActions.rationale: SpO2 is currently 96% — no supplemental O2 required, but apply nasal cannula prophylactically and titrate.

expectedActions.shortAction: Acquire 12-lead EKG (afib confirmed; rule out STEMI mimics)
expectedActions.rationale: Atrial fibrillation is the likely embolic source; also rules out concurrent STEMI.

expectedActions.shortAction: Do NOT treat the elevated BP (184/102)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Notes/Educational Pearls: Do not treat hypertension.
expectedActions.rationale: Common error: aggressively lowering BP in acute stroke worsens penumbral ischemia. Hospital decides any BP management.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Prevent aspiration – elevate head of stretcher 15–30 degrees if systolic BP greater than 100 mmHg. Maintain head and neck in neutral alignment, without flexing the neck.
expectedActions.rationale: Reduces aspiration risk in dysphagic stroke patient while preserving cerebral perfusion.

expectedActions.shortAction: Early stroke alert with LVO findings to thrombectomy-capable center
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: Early hospital notification per local stroke plan that should include any suspected large vessel occlusion (LVO) stroke.
expectedActions.rationale: Pre-arrival activation directly reduces door-to-needle and door-to-groin times.

transitions.if: LVO recognized; transport to thrombectomy center
transitions.condition: Crew completes stroke and severity assessments, recognizes positive LVO findings (forced gaze, global aphasia, dense hemiparesis), and elects to bypass the closer Primary Stroke Center for the Comprehensive/Thrombectomy-capable Stroke Center 22 minutes away with early notification.
Crew transports to the closest Primary Stroke Center without recognizing or communicating LVO severity findings. Patient will require secondary transfer for thrombectomy, losing valuable time.

phases.name: LVO recognized; transport
phases.description: Crew has identified LVO features and elected to bypass the closest hospital for the thrombectomy-capable Comprehensive Stroke Center, with early notification. Patient is transported with HOB elevated, on continuous monitoring, with no aggressive BP intervention. Symptoms remain stable en route.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew identified LVO severity findings, made a destination decision to bypass to a thrombectomy-capable center, and gave early hospital notification with the suspected LVO designation.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent and self-maintained; head of stretcher elevated 30°
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Irregularly irregular pulse; warm dry skin
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Equal, reactive; left gaze deviation persists
otherFindings: Patient cooperative with stretcher movement and turn

expectedActions.shortAction: Single IV attempt, large-bore (avoid multiple attempts)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Avoid multiple IV attempts.
expectedActions.rationale: Multiple IV attempts cause hematomas that interfere with thrombolytic therapy decision-making.

expectedActions.shortAction: Minimize on-scene time; expedite to receiving hospital
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: Minimize EMS scene time.
expectedActions.rationale: Time is brain — every minute lost reduces salvageable tissue and worsens outcome.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pertinent historical data includes: Neurologic status assessment.
expectedActions.rationale: Identifies hemorrhagic conversion (sudden BP rise, vomiting, decreased LOC) requiring change in destination if needed.

expectedActions.shortAction: Protect the paretic right arm/leg from injury during moves
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Protect paralyzed limbs from injury.
expectedActions.rationale: Flaccid limbs are easily injured during stretcher transfers.

phases.name: Closest hospital — no LVO triage
phases.description: Crew transports to the closer Primary Stroke Center, missing the LVO findings or not communicating them. The patient will need a secondary transfer for thrombectomy, costing 60-90 minutes of door-to-puncture time and reducing the chance of meaningful recovery. This phase exists to teach the consequence of skipping the severity scale.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew did not perform a stroke severity scale (or did not act on positive LVO findings) and transported to the nearest stroke-ready center. Receiving facility will need to arrange a secondary transfer; time-to-thrombectomy is now significantly delayed.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Irregularly irregular pulse; warm dry skin
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Equal, reactive; left gaze deviation persists
otherFindings: Symptoms unchanged on arrival at the closer hospital
Receiving ED begins immediate workup but cannot provide thrombectomy
Secondary transfer process initiated; estimated 60-90 minute delay to thrombectomy-capable center

expectedActions.shortAction: Perform severity scale and notify receiving of LVO suspicion
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Use a validated prehospital stroke severity scale that may include, but is not limited to: Vision changes; Sensory neglect; Aphasia.
expectedActions.rationale: Even after arrival at the wrong center, communicating LVO findings expedites the secondary transfer process.

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize destination decision: thrombectomy-capable bypass was indicated
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Notes/Educational Pearls: Transport and destination decisions should be based on local resources and stroke system of care. Destination hospitals may include: Stroke Ready; Primary Stroke Center; Thrombectomy-capable Stroke Center; Comprehensive Stroke Center. Positive stroke severity scale with time of onset or last known well less than 24 hours may be eligible for mechanical thrombectomy.
expectedActions.rationale: Closing the loop with the crew on the destination decision is the entire teaching point of this branch.
